title: Tax Incidence and Deadweight Loss
description: Practice what you've learned about tax incidence and deadweight loss when a tax is placed on a market in this exercise.
topic_path: Consumer and producer surplus, market interventions, and international trade

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shown represents a market in which a per unit tax has been imposed.

**What is the dollar amount of the tax, and who has the tax been imposed on?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: When a per unit tax is imposed on a market, the supply curve will decrease (shift *up* vertically) by the amount of the tax.

Note that the initial supply curve began at a price of $\$2$, and the new supply curve begins at a price of $\$7$. 

$\$7 - \$2 = \$5$
A $\$5$ per unit tax has been imposed on this market

options.alt: A line graph shows the effects of a tax on a market model, illustrating the relationship between price, P, on the Y-axis and quantity, Q, on the X-axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 13 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 7 with increments of 1. Three lines are displayed: The demand line, labeled D, slopes downward from left to right between (0, 13) and (8, 5). The initial supply line, labeled S 1, slopes upward from left to right between (0, 2) and (7, 9). The supply line after the tax, labeled S tax, slopes upward from left to right, above S 1 between (0, 2) and (7, 8). The D line intersects S 1 at (5.5, 7.5) and S tax at (3, 10). Dashed lines connect the points of intersection to the axes. There are three shaded areas between 1 and 3 on the horizontal axis. Area 1 is between (0, 13), (0, 10), and (3, 10). Area 2 is between (0, 10), (0, 5), (3, 10), and (3, 5). Area 3 is between (0, 2), (0, 5), and (3, 5).
options.caption: A market in which a per-unit tax has been imposed on the sellers of a good

assessment_items.question_content:
Assume a market has a downward sloping demand curve and an upward sloping supply curve. 

**Which of the following statements about the impact of a per unit tax on this market is NOT true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When a per unit tax is imposed on a market, the price that buyers pay will always increase by the amount of the tax
The more the quantity sold in the market decreases, the larger the deadweight loss will be
The tax burden is the consumer and producer surplus that becomes government revenue
Deadweight loss is the surplus that is lost from buyers and sellers that are no longer able to participate in the market
Unless both supply and demand are perfectly inelastic, a tax results in deadweight loss.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below shows the effect of a $\$1$ tax on a good with a perfectly elastic supply.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the tax revenue collected when this $\$1$ per unit tax was imposed on this market?**


**What is the total amount of tax revenue and how much of that is the producer's tax burden?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If a $\$1$ per unit tax is levied, each of the units sold will be taxed.
According to this graph, the intersection of the supply curve with the tax and the demand curve is at a quantity of $3$
Therefore, a tax revenue of $\$1 \times 3 = \$3$ is collected
The producer burden is the original price minus the price producers get to keep times the quantity sold
The price consumers pay increases by the full $\$1$ and producers still get to keep the price they were charging before the tax, $\$7$
The producer tax burden is $\$7-\$7=\$0$. The entire tax burden is placed on consumers, for whom the price increases by the full $\$1$ per unit

options.alt: A line graph shows the effects of a tax on a market model, illustrating the relationship between price, P and quantity, Q. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 11 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 11 with increments of 1. Three lines are displayed: The demand line, labeled D, slopes downward from left to right between (0, 11) and (11, 0). The initial supply line, labeled S 1, is horizontal at P equals 7. The supply line after the tax, labeled S tax, is horizontal at P equals 8, above S 1. The D line intersects S 1 at (4, 7) and S tax at (3, 8).
options.caption: A $\$1$ per unit tax has been imposed on sellers of this good

choices.content: Tax revenue is $\$3$; producer's tax burden is $\$0$.
Tax revenue is $\$3$; producer's tax burden is $\$3$
Tax revenue is $\$3$; producer's tax burden is $\$1.50$.
Tax revenue is $\$1$; producer's tax burden is $\$1$;
Tax revenue is $\$1$; producer's tax burden is $\$0$;

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph illustrates a good where a tax has been imposed on the buyers of that good.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the consumer's tax incidence and what is the producer's tax incidence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To find the tax incidence of a buyer, look for the area that is under the price that the buyer now has to pay, and above the price that they used to pay before the tax, at the quantity that they now buy.
Here, the buyer used tp pay a price of $\$11$ and now pays a price of $\$12$, and buys $4$ units of this good.
Consumer's tax burden is:
$(\$12 - \$11) \times 4 = \$4$
To find the tax incidence of a seller, look for the area that is under the price that the buyer used to receive, and above the price that they now get after the tax at the quantity that they now sell.
Here, the seller used to get a price of $\$11$ and now gets a price of $\$9$, and sells $4$ units of this good.
Producer surplus:

$(\$11 - \$9) \times 4 = \$8$

options.alt: A line graph shows the effects of a tax on a market model, illustrating the relationship between price, P, on the Y-axis and quantity, Q, on the X-axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 16 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 7 with increments of 1. Three lines are displayed, the demand line, labeled D, slopes downward from left to right, the initial supply line, labeled S 1, slopes upward from left to right, and the demand line after the tax, labeled D tax, slopes upward from left to right below S 1. The D line intersects S 1 at coordinates (5, 11) and D t at coordinates (4, 9). Dashed lines connect points and their projections to the axes, creating a grid. Shaded areas are labeled as follows, R above T, S between T and W, U below W, and V below S 1 and to the left of W.
options.caption: A tax on buyers of a good

choices.content: Consumer's tax incidence is $\$4$; producer's tax incidence is $\$8$.
Consumer's tax incidence is $\$12$; producer's tax incidence is $\$0$
Consumer's tax incidence is $\$6$; producer's tax incidence is $\$6$
Consumer's tax incidence is $\$0$; producer's tax incidence is $\$12$
Consumer's tax incidence is $\$8$; producer's tax incidence is $\$4$

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows a market where a tax has been imposed on the buyers of a good.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What letters represent the tax burden of sellers?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph illustrates the effects of a tax on a market model, showing the relationship between price, P, on the Y-axis and quantity, Q, on the X-axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 17 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 7 with increments of 1. Four lines are displayed, the demand line, labeled D, slopes downward from left to right, the initial supply line, labeled S 1, slopes upward from left to right, the taxed demand line, labeled D t, slopes downward below D, and the marginal cost line labeled S intersects the other lines. Key intersections include D and S 1 at (5, 12), D t and S at (4, 10). Shaded areas are labeled A, B, E, and F and represent distinct surplus and tax regions.
options.caption: A market with a tax on buyers of a good

choices.content: None, since the buyers of a good were taxed
